museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: ColecoVision begins its limited rollout, Tron reaches cinemas, and the year's home-arcade promise starts to look more competitive.
museum.curatorNote: July's strongest hardware anchor is ColecoVision's late-July limited rollout in New York and Boston. Tron is included as game-adjacent arcade/media context rather than a precise game release claim.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: July turns arcade culture into a hardware and cinema story.

items.title: ColecoVision begins limited rollout
items.body: ColecoVision launched around the end of July in New York and Boston, expanding during August and later months with Donkey Kong as its pack-in showpiece.
items.sourceNote: Atari Archive cites Computer Entertainer: late July in New York/Boston, expansion in August, national rollout later.

items.title: Tron brings arcade imagery to cinema
items.body: Disney's Tron made videogame imagery part of a major film object, even if the arcade tie-ins and release timings vary by market.
items.certainty: film release/context verified; game date not asserted
items.sourceNote: Tron film released in 1982; arcade-game timing is handled broadly to avoid false precision.

items.title: Donkey Kong becomes console bait
items.body: Coleco's use of Donkey Kong as a pack-in made one arcade licence feel like a reason to choose hardware.
items.sourceNote: ColecoVision launched with Donkey Kong as pack-in.

items.title: Spectrum software ecosystem grows
items.body: Summer Spectrum ownership meant more tapes, more adverts and more small publishers trying to be found.

items.title: Atari faces a more credible home challenger
items.body: The Atari 2600 still dominated, but ColecoVision's visual comparison pitch made the home market feel less settled.
items.sourceNote: ColecoVision launch and Donkey Kong pack-in context.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: July release history is really rollout history.

items.title: ColecoVision
items.body: Limited-market launch begins with Donkey Kong as the defining pack-in.

items.title: Donkey Kong
items.body: The pack-in that made the new console legible to arcade players.
items.sourceNote: ColecoVision launched with Donkey Kong pack-in.

items.title: Pac-Man
items.body: Still the mass-market cartridge everyone could compare against better-looking arcade promises.

items.title: Ms. Pac-Man
items.body: The arcade sequel stayed strong through summer.

items.title: Dig Dug
items.body: A spring release now established in arcades.

items.title: Robotron: 2084
items.body: A cabinet that made control itself the attraction.

items.title: Zaxxon
items.body: Still a useful comparison point for claims of arcade-quality home graphics.

items.title: Spectrum cassette releases
items.body: The names are harder to date precisely, but the flow of small UK cassette software was now part of the market.

items.title: ColecoVision
items.body: A home console sold on the idea that arcade conversions could look closer to the real thing.

items.title: Atari 2600 under pressure
items.body: The 2600's huge base still mattered, but its technical limits were easier to see beside Coleco's new pitch.

items.title: Tron-style arcade spectacle
items.body: The arcade was increasingly part of wider pop-culture imagery, not just an amusement venue.

items.title: Spectrum tape setup
items.body: UK home gaming still involved a TV, a computer, a cassette recorder and patience.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: July magazines had new hardware to explain.

items.title: ColecoVision preview culture
items.headline: A new home-arcade challenger
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games cover for July 1982.
items.curatorNote: ColecoVision's promise was exactly the kind of hardware story magazines could turn into charts and screenshots.
items.body: ColecoVision's promise was exactly the kind of hardware story magazines could turn into charts and screenshots.

items.title: UK micro weekly coverage
items.headline: Spectrum software and small adverts
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Popular Computing Weekly cover for July 1982.
items.curatorNote: For UK readers, the weekly press made new software feel findable.
items.body: For UK readers, the weekly press made new software feel findable.

items.title: C&VG summer mix
items.headline: Arcade spectacle and home machine choice
items.curatorNote: C&VG could cover Tron-like arcade spectacle and bedroom computers in the same breath.
items.body: C&VG could cover Tron-like arcade spectacle and bedroom computers in the same breath.

items.title: Personal Computer World
items.headline: Choosing a home computer
items.curatorNote: The serious buying-guide voice mattered because computers were still expensive family decisions.
items.body: The serious buying-guide voice mattered because computers were still expensive family decisions.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: The market's network was still retail and print.

items.title: Comparison charts were search engines
items.body: Magazines helped readers compare machines they could not easily try.

items.title: Adverts made small software visible
items.body: A tiny classified could launch a buying decision.

items.title: Arcade gossip had geography
items.body: What you knew depended on which machines your local venue had.

items.title: Cinema fed the arcade imagination
items.body: Tron made game imagery feel part of a larger future.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: July felt like a new challenger stepping into a noisy room.

items.title: Donkey Kong sold more than a game
items.body: As a pack-in, it sold the idea that home could get closer to arcade.

items.title: Atari suddenly looked older
items.body: The 2600 was familiar, but familiarity could become age.

items.title: The Spectrum made another route
items.body: Not arcade perfect, but personal, cheap and programmable.

items.title: The future had competing shapes
items.body: Console, computer, cabinet and cinema all claimed part of play.

sources.label: ColecoVision launch chronology
sources.note: Used for late July limited rollout and Donkey Kong pack-in.

sources.label: ColecoVision overview
sources.note: Used for August launch and Donkey Kong pack-in context.

sources.label: Tron game-adjacent context
sources.note: Used for 1982 film context; no exact game release day asserted.
